guest_role: Partner at Redpoint Ventures, author of 'Winning with Data'
assertiveness_why: Harry asks substantive, well-prepared follow-ups and occasionally voices his own reservation (on data vs. human emotion in hiring), but never truly contests Tunguz's claims and stays in fan/receiving mode throughout.
question_style: Mostly open-ended, topic-seeded questions built off specific passages in the book, with natural follow-ups that build on the prior answer rather than pure Q&A; Harry occasionally interjects a personal reservation (e.g. on emotion vs. data in hiring) but frames it gently rather than as a challenge, and he does not interrupt or contest Tunguz's claims.
tone: Warm, matey, and openly fannish (repeatedly praises the book as one of the best he's read in years); light joking rapport (an aside referencing Jason Lemkin) and clear familiarity from a prior appearance.
depth: Goes reasonably deep into specific book concepts — data teams/data dictionary/data pipeline, structured interviewing stats (Adam Grant), and named cognitive biases (anchoring, availability, illusion of validity, Monty Hall problem) — tracking each thread through concrete examples rather than staying purely surface-level.
harry_quotes: Why does it take so long to write the book?
Talk to me about the availability bias.
Is there a way to prevent this kind of false sense of knowledge?
learnings: The best data-driven companies share three structures: a dedicated data team focused on education, a single data dictionary that standardizes metric definitions across departments, and a data pipeline/supply chain that gets data to frontline employees quickly.

Unstructured interviews predict job success only slightly better than chance, but pairing structured, consistent interview questions with a standardized cognitive assessment can push predictive accuracy up dramatically.
Recruiting speed itself is a data point worth tracking — shortening the time from first contact to offer materially improves hiring outcomes.

Pure data-driven decision-making without emotion doesn't work in practice; emotion helps people act under uncertainty when information is incomplete, so successful hiring and leadership decisions still need human judgment and debate alongside the numbers.

People systematically overrate their ability to predict outcomes (illusion of validity) and overweight vivid, memorable events (availability bias); running small experiments to test assumptions is the practical antidote.
Company culture becomes data-driven when leadership asks open-ended questions and expects answers backed by data and argument rather than opinion, cascading the behavior both top-down and bottom-up.
repeat_guest_callback: Tom so fantastic to have you on the show for a second time this obviously makes you a very special guest
notable: Harry explicitly breaks his stated rule against reading books on air, calling this the first book he's finished in about two years, underscoring his personal enthusiasm for the guest.
